Patch Set 6: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

You updated the commit comment, but it doesn't seem that you committed the actual changes to the test program or conf-sanity?

Minor note - it probably isn't necessary to list the actual test program in the commit comment since it is already in the commit itself.
Uploaded patch set 7.

Patch Set 7:

Build Started http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11950/

Patch Set 7: Verified

Build Successful 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11950/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 7:

Autotest has started the test process

Patch Set 7: Fails

Tests received by maloo, run on el6/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/6db5400a-5c4d-11e2-aa66-52540035b04c).  Ran 2 tests.  1 tests failed: lustre-initialization-1.

Patch Set 7:

A good addition.  It seems more natural to fail the build if a commit breaks the user space compiling or linking.  Is there any reason why we do not build the test program in the build process and run it at testing time?

Patch Set 7: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Li Wei makes a good point - why wait until the test is run (_if_ it is run locally by the developer) to detect build failure, when it could be caught during the build itself?  Catching that at build time takes a lot less resources.

It probably makes sense to add some comments to the test itself that makes it clear the "fix" for a build breakage is NOT to change the test program, but rather to ensure that the test program does not break in the first place.

Patch Set 7:

I think that it may be somewhat harder to test this at normal build time.  You'll need to be very careful to scrub the build line of all of the lustre internal includes and such.  After all, we've failed to notice for a long time that lustre_idl.h doesn't build in user space, because it builds fine as part of the lustre build system.

So I think thats what Richard was trying to do here, build it more independently so we catch those problems in the future.

Patch Set 7:

I think Chris's point is valid.  We need to test building the user space program against _installed_ headers and libraries.  And, it would be difficult and less convincing to simulate so at Lustre build time.

Richard, did you try the test in-tree (i.e., without "make install" or "rpm --install")?  The test should either skip or provide additional compiling and linking arguments to locate the headers and libraries.

Patch Set 7:

I suspect one reason that lustre_idl.h didn't fail compiling in the past is because it was always used in conjunction with other Lustre headers in lctl.c and lfs.c, and not in a "stand-alone" manner as in the test program.

I haven't looked specifically, so I don't know if there are headers explicitly included or symbols defined from the build command line. If there are no such command-line enhancements, then it should be ok to test this at build time using a simple test program like this one.

Patch Set 7: No score

parents.subject: LU-3006 utils: mount to pass/clear UPDATE flag

commit.subject: LU-1606 api: compile and build lustreapi test
commit.message:
LU-1606 api: compile and build lustreapi test

To avoid regression of the client api conf-sanity includes a new test
73 to verify the lustreapi can be compiled and linked against. All the
files in the directory $LUSTRE_TESTS_API_DIR with the extension .c are
compiled and linked. The directory is located in the build tree at
./lustre/tests/clientapi.

Signed-off-by: Richard Henwood <richard.henwood@intel.com>
Change-Id: I0ad6a1671bf7033ec2ad5bc7a82d82e468cd31c2
